textPlain: I had a similar idea when apple vision pro came out, to be able to code while laying on a couch or bed fully relaxed, but never got to doing it. Neat!
Very nice work. Might not hurt to throw in a few more pictures that illustrate the steps in the build process.

Not a new thing. 
Twiddler is on version 4. 
https://www.mytwiddler.com/

Dude, this is so cool! I've had something like this floating around my cluttered headspace for ages, and it always sort of floats to the surface during the rare times when I do some light sysadmin work via Termux on my phone.Another thing that jumps to mind is the minichord[1], a nominally open-source synth/instrument.I just love seeing these little devices people can come up with given the proliferation of the necessary devboards and tools. Nice project.
Nice work! I'm happy to see that some folks are still interested in wearable computing.The Twiddler gives me hand cramps, so I might give this one a shot. (It's low on the pile, however.)

There was a product very similar to this in the late 1980s. My uncle invested in the company. But it flopped. I am sorry I cannot remember the name. I do remember skilled typists could use it at least as fast as 2 hand traditional typing.

That tracks. I just don't think there's the sales volume you'd need to manufacture and sell something like this at an adequate profit, and certainly in the 80s it would have been even more difficult to find enough compute and utility in one place without the benefit of Internet services.
